title: APPENDIX — Consolidated summary and reading list

lines.text: APPENDIX — Consolidated summary and reading list
The central recipe.
Tissue engineering is scaffold plus cells plus bioactives.
The scaffold provides three-dimensional form.
The cells provide biological function.
The bioactives — growth factors, signaling molecules, mechanical and electrical cues — direct cell behavior.
3D bioprinting is the technology layer that arranges these three ingredients with micrometer-scale spatial precision.
Founding figures.
Robert Langer (MIT chemical engineer) and Joseph Vacanti (Harvard transplant surgeon) named the field with their 1993 Science paper "Tissue Engineering.
" Their paradigm — scaffold plus cells plus bioactives — has structured every subsequent textbook chapter, grant application, and conference talk in the field.
Langer's lab has spun out over forty biotech companies.
Vacanti's "ear on a mouse" remains the field's iconic public image.
The early clinical wins.
Epicel (1980s) — cultured autologous epidermis for burn patients, developed from Howard Green's keratinocyte culture work.
First clinical use in 1981 in two boys with 95% burns.
Carticel (1995–1997) — cultured autologous chondrocytes for cartilage defects, from Peterson and Brittberg.
First FDA-approved cell therapy.
Atala's bladder (2006) — first tissue-engineered organ implanted in humans.
Patients with spina bifida received bladders grown from their own urothelial and smooth muscle cells on a custom collagen/PGA scaffold.
Published in The Lancet.
The cautionary tale.
Paolo Macchiarini, a Karolinska Institute surgeon, implanted decellularized and synthetic tracheas in patients during the 2010s.
Most patients died.
Investigation revealed falsified outcomes data.
Multiple papers retracted.
Karolinska leadership resigned.
Macchiarini was criminally prosecuted.
The case illustrates what happens when clinical translation outruns preclinical evidence, particularly under press and institutional pressure.
Decellularization.

Harald Ott and Doris Taylor's 2008 Nature Medicine paper demonstrated the "ghost heart" — a rat heart chemically stripped of cells, leaving the ECM scaffold, then reseeded with new cells and made to beat.
Whole-organ recellularization has since been attempted for heart, liver, kidney, and lung, with partial success at best.
The scaffolds are beautiful.
The recellularization step remains the bottleneck.
Scaffold materials.
Natural polymers — collagen, alginate, gelatin, hyaluronic acid, fibrin, decellularized ECM — offer biological compatibility but limited mechanical control.
Synthetic polymers — PLGA, PCL, PEG — offer reproducibility and tunability but require biological coatings.
Most modern scaffolds are hybrid.
Hydrogels — especially GelMA, gelatin methacrylate — dominate bioprinting bio-inks.
Bioprinting technologies.
Extrusion (most common, moderate resolution, high cell density).
Inkjet (fast, higher resolution, lower cell density).
Laser-induced forward transfer (high resolution, slow, expensive).
Stereolithography / DLP (high resolution, fast, photopolymer chemistry only).
The resolution problem.
Cells are ~10 micrometers.
Bioprinter voxels are ~100 micrometers.
The biology is finer than the printing tool.
Partial solutions include reliance on cell-level self-organization within printed deposits, higher-resolution printing technologies, and post-print maturation in bioreactors.
The vascularization wall.
Cells need to be within ~200 micrometers of a blood vessel to survive.
Thicker engineered tissues require internal vasculature.
Strategies include pre-vascularization with co-cultured endothelial cells, printable internal channels (Lewis-style sacrificial printing), and vascularized organoid assembly.
The vascularization problem is the single largest obstacle to organ-scale tissue engineering.
Jennifer Lewis's sacrificial printing.
Print Pluronic F-127 filaments in the shape of a vascular tree.
Cast hydrogel around them.
Cool to liquefy Pluronic.
Flow the Pluronic out, leaving hollow channels.
Line with endothelial cells.
Perfuse.
This technique has produced the most complex vascularized printed tissues to date.
Commercial bioprinting.
Organovo pioneered printed liver tissue for drug testing in the 2010s; commercial difficulties followed.
Current major players in bioprinters and bio-inks include CELLINK / BICO, Aspect Biosystems, RegenHU, Allevi, and Advanced Solutions.
The customer base is largely pharmaceutical and academic.
Bioprinted skin.
Multi-layer constructs of fibroblasts in dermal hydrogel plus keratinocytes on top, sometimes with melanocytes, vascular cells, and immune cells.
Used for burn replacement, chronic wounds, and drug/cosmetics testing.
The most clinically translated bioprinting application.
Bioprinted cornea.
Small, thin, avascular tissue — ideally matched to current bioprinting capabilities.
Several research demonstrations, with clinical translation in progress.
Likely to be one of the next approved bioprinted tissue products.
Bioprinted meat.
Same recipe — cells, scaffold, bioactives, sometimes bioprinting — applied to producing meat from cultured animal cells.
Aleph Farms, Believer Meats, UPSIDE Foods, Mosa Meat are major players.
Regulatory approval in Singapore (2020), U.
S.
Cost reduction remains the main commercial obstacle.
The frontier.
In-vivo bioprinting — printing directly onto wounds and into surgical defects.
Vascularized organoids — combining organoid self-organization with printed vasculature.
Organ-on-chip integration — bioprinting into microfluidic devices for drug screening.
AI-driven scaffold design — machine learning for bio-ink formulation and scaffold geometry optimization.
Long-viability bio-inks — materials that support cell survival and maturation over months.
One-sentence summary.

Tissue engineering combines biocompatible scaffolds, patient-derived cells, and bioactive cues to build replacement tissues; 3D bioprinting adds spatial precision through layer-by-layer deposition of cell-laden hydrogels; clinical wins exist for skin, cartilage, bladder, and cornea; the central unsolved problem is vascularization at organ scale; the field is making real but incremental progress toward printable replacement organs.
Short reading list.
Langer R, Vacanti JP.
"Tissue Engineering.
The founding paper.
Atala A et al.
"Tissue-engineered autologous bladders for patients needing cystoplasty.
The first engineered organ in humans.
Ott HC et al.
"Perfusion-decellularized matrix: using nature's platform to engineer a bioartificial heart.
" Nature Medicine 14:213-221 (2008).
The ghost heart paper.
Kolesky DB, Homan KA, Skylar-Scott MA, Lewis JA.
"Three-dimensional bioprinting of thick vascularized tissues.
Lewis sacrificial printing for thick vascularized tissues.
Murphy SV, Atala A.
"3D bioprinting of tissues and organs.
" Nature Biotechnology 32:773-785 (2014).
A widely cited bioprinting review.
For the Macchiarini story — the Swedish investigative documentary "Experimenten" (2016), and Adam Ciralsky's reporting in Vanity Fair (2016).
End of lecture.
